cataloging_subject.label: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The late-medieval Middle Scots verse work conventionally attributed to Robert Henryson and represented as a 13-fable sequence with two prologues and a Moralitas after every narrative. Single_work treats that sequence as the present cataloging unit; it does not decide the scholarly debate over whether all 13 fables were conceived as one authorially ordered collection. The abstract work remains distinct from its early prints and manuscripts, Smith's five-stream 1906 edition, the OTA and U-M encodings, and the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
catalog_summary.author: Robert Henryson
catalog_summary.editor: G. Gregory Smith (1906 source edition); George Stevenson transcribed and collated its five source streams
catalog_summary.region: Scotland; Henryson is associated with Dunfermline, but the exact place of composition is unproved

titles.value: The Morall Fabillis of Esope the Phrygian
notes: The spelling follows the established early-print and encoded title tradition; it is not claimed as an authorial autograph title.

agents.name: Robert Henryson
notes: A poet and notary associated with Dunfermline; biographical evidence is sparse, and he was dead by about 1500.

The attribution is sustained across early witnesses and prints and accepted by current scholarship. Aesop is a named traditional authority and source association, not a coauthor of the Middle Scots work.

scope.description: 1906 printed source representation, not medieval authorship

notes: Smith edited the five early source streams printed in the edition from which the electronic lineage descends.

scope.description: source transcription and collation for Smith's 1906 edition

notes: Smith states that Stevenson transcribed and collated all five texts.
The range is deliberately conservative. The repository XML's unsupported legacy date 1450 is not adopted; OTA's 1480-1489 record is useful but not by itself conclusive.

date_statements.display: Fragments survive in witnesses written or augmented in the early sixteenth century
notes: Makculloch preserves the prologue and Cock and Jasp material; Asloan preserves The Two Mice. This is a witness date, not a composition date.

date_statements.display: The earliest surviving edition has a colophon dated 16 December 1569 and a title page dated 1570
notes: Smith speculated about a lost earlier Chepman and Myllar printing but stated that evidence was entirely wanting; it is not promoted to an event here.
Henryson is documented as a notary and teacher at Dunfermline in 1477-1478. This association is not automatically a place-of-composition claim.
OTA's legacy code gives ?FIFE, and Henryson's Dunfermline association makes Fife plausible, but no direct composition evidence was found.

scope.description: short maxims, names, and embedded learned phrases scattered through the work

form_statements.description: The work is stanzaic verse, predominantly seven-line rhyme royal (ababbcc); the encoded base also contains seven eight-line stanzas.

subject_statements.label: Animals as human social and moral figures
Social estates, law, exploitation, and institutional corruption

summaries.value: A sequence of thirteen Middle Scots beast fables in which animal narratives open into explicit moral interpretation. Henryson combines Aesopic storytelling with satire, theology, legal and social criticism, and reflection on prudence, power, poverty, justice, and human appetite. A general prologue explains the value of mingling serious doctrine with merry fiction, and a second prologue before The Lion and the Mouse stages an encounter with Aesop.

The represented order is: general prologue; Cock and Jasp; Two Mice; Cock and Fox; Fox's Confession to Friar Wolf; Trial or Parliament of Beasts; Sheep and Dog; second prologue and Lion and Mouse; Preaching of the Swallow; Fox, Wolf, and Cadger; Fox, Wolf, and Shadow of the Moon; Wolf and Wether; Wolf and Lamb; and Paddock and Mouse. Every fable narrative is followed by a Moralitas.

The repository representation is one continuous 2,975-line text whose 109 page markers follow Smith's Harleian odd pages 3-219, but its title is Bassandyne 1571 and at least one unnumbered stanza comes from a stream absent in Harleian. Counts describe the XML and its inherited lineation, not a new critical edition.

extent.description: The XML represents 13 fable narratives, 13 moralities, and 2 prologues: 28 intellectual sections containing 424 stanza groups and 2,975 line elements. Of these, 2,968 lines carry inherited numbers; a seven-line stanza in the Cock and Fox Moralitas is unnumbered. Two numbered lines sit directly outside LG. The intellectual allocation is 21 prologue stanzas/147 lines, 324 narrative stanzas/2,268 lines, and 79 morality stanzas/560 lines.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: A defense of morally useful fiction and a declaration of translation from Aesop into the mother tongue.

content_parts.label: The Cock and the Jasp
content_parts.summary: A cock finds a jewel but values food above a treasure he cannot use.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Cock and the Jasp
content_parts.summary: The jewel signifies prudence and learning, while the cock figures contempt for knowledge.

content_parts.label: The Uponland Mouse and the Burgess Mouse (The Two Mice)
content_parts.summary: Country and town mice test one another's ways of life and contrast humble security with anxious abundance.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Two Mice
content_parts.summary: The safer contentment of a simple life is preferred to wealth exposed to continual fear.

content_parts.label: Schir Chantecleir and the Fox
content_parts.summary: A fox captures a cock through flattery and loses him when tricked into speaking.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: Schir Chantecleir and the Fox
content_parts.summary: The tale warns against flattery, pride, and heedless speech.

locators.value: HenFabl:4.1; numbered lines 586-606 plus one unnumbered seven-line stanza

notes: The unnumbered stanza beginning 'Fy puft vp pryde' is present in the Charteris column of Smith but absent from the facing Harleian column, exposing cross-stream supplementation in the electronic text.

content_parts.label: The Fox's Confession to Friar Wolf Waitskaith
content_parts.summary: The fox confesses to a corrupt wolf friar whose counsel fails to reform appetite or predation.
notes: Line 615 is a direct DIV1 child outside an LG, an encoding boundary anomaly rather than evidence of prose.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Fox's Confession
content_parts.summary: The morality attacks hypocritical confession and corrupt spiritual guidance.

content_parts.label: The Trial of the Fox / Parliament of Four-footed Beasts
content_parts.summary: The fox's heir is summoned before the lion's parliament in an extended satire of law, court, and predatory power.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Trial of the Fox
content_parts.summary: The animal court is applied to human estates, false counsel, and institutional injustice.

content_parts.label: The Sheep and the Dog
content_parts.summary: A sheep loses a legal case to a dog through false witnesses and a corrupt court.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Sheep and the Dog
content_parts.summary: The morality condemns perverted law, bribery, oppression, and false testimony.

content_parts.label: Prologue to The Lion and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: A dream-vision encounter with Aesop introduces the following fable and reflects on poetic authority.

content_parts.label: The Lion and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: A mouse spared by a lion later frees the lion from a net, demonstrating reciprocal mercy across unequal power.
notes: The XML mislabels HenFabl:8.2 as a second prologue; its contents are the fable narrative.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Lion and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: The morality commends mercy and warns rulers not to despise the weak.

content_parts.label: The Preaching of the Swallow
content_parts.summary: A swallow's repeatedly ignored warnings about flax and snares culminate in the destruction of the other birds.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Preaching of the Swallow
content_parts.summary: The morality urges timely attention to wise teaching and spiritual warning.

content_parts.label: The Fox, the Wolf, and the Cadger's Nekhering
content_parts.summary: The fox tricks a wolf into pursuing a herring cart, and the wolf suffers for greed and credulity.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Nekhering
content_parts.summary: The morality exposes appetite, deception, and the hazards of corrupt companionship.

content_parts.label: The Fox, the Wolf, and the Shadow of the Moon
content_parts.summary: The fox persuades the hungry wolf that the moon's reflection is cheese and exploits his gullibility.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Shadow of the Moon
content_parts.summary: The morality warns against fantasy, greed, and reliance on deceitful advisers.

content_parts.label: The Wolf and the Wether
content_parts.summary: A wether wearing a dog's skin briefly frightens a wolf before his disguise and weakness are exposed.
notes: Line 2475 is a direct DIV1 child outside an LG, an encoding boundary anomaly rather than prose.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Wolf and the Wether
content_parts.summary: The morality attacks false appearance, presumption, and those who assume offices beyond their capacity.

content_parts.label: The Wolf and the Lamb
content_parts.summary: A wolf invents successive accusations against an innocent lamb and kills him despite reasoned legal defense.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Wolf and the Lamb
content_parts.summary: The lamb figures laborers and the poor, while wolves figure corrupt lawyers, lords, landlords, and extortioners.

content_parts.label: The Paddock and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: A deceitful frog binds herself to a mouse in crossing water; a kite seizes and kills both adversaries.

content_parts.label: Moralitas: The Paddock and the Mouse
content_parts.summary: The concluding morality warns against false fellowship and allegorizes body, soul, world, and sudden death.

related_works.title: The Aesopic fable tradition
notes: Henryson explicitly names Aesop as his authority, and scholarship identifies multiple Latin and vernacular analogues. This broad relation avoids falsely selecting one surviving physical exemplar as the direct source for the entire collection.

related_works.title: Verse Romulus / Isopet associated with Gualterus Anglicus
notes: A major source tradition for several fables, not an assertion that all thirteen derive directly and exclusively from one fixed copy or recension.

evidence.citation: General Corpus HenFabl source XML and structural inspection
evidence.summary: Verifies work title, represented order, 13 narratives, 13 moralities, two prologues, section labels, exact stanza and line counts, language, themes, malformed local nesting, page markers, and the unnumbered cross-stream stanza.
Binds abstract-work claims to the repository XML, U-M and OTA encodings, Smith edition, early prints, manuscripts, transcripts, complete digital objects, access states, and rights layers.

evidence.citation: G. Gregory Smith, ed., The Poems of Robert Henryson, Text—Vol. I (Scottish Text Society 55, 1906), 1968 Johnson facsimile reprint
evidence.locator: prefatory note pp. vii-xiv; contents pp. xx-xxi; Fables pp. 2-327
evidence.summary: Verifies Henryson attribution, five early source streams, their contents and extent, Smith and Stevenson's roles, early-print dates, represented 13-fable order, and U-M page mapping.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, The Morall Fabillis: Introduction
evidence.summary: Provides current scholarship on Henryson, date context, title and genre, all principal witnesses and prints, textual relationships, contents, and the debate over collection unity and order.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, earlier scholarly introduction to The Morall Fabillis
evidence.summary: Surveys possible Latin and vernacular source traditions, including Gualterus Anglicus, Lydgate, Caxton, Petrus Alfonsi, Roman de Renart, Chaucer, and Odo, without establishing one direct exemplar for the whole work.

evidence.citation: ARLIMA, Robert Henryson, The Morall Fabillis of Esope
evidence.summary: Confirms conventional title and attribution, Scots fable classification, late-fifteenth-century dating, 13-fable and 2,975-line extent, and early-print bibliography.

evidence.citation: University of Glasgow, The Moral Fables: Introduction
evidence.summary: Supports Middle Scots language, 13-tale design with moralities, beast-fable genre, and broad mid-to-late-fifteenth-century context.
notes: The exact current University of Glasgow page route should be rechecked if this citation is refreshed; claims are corroborated independently by METS and the XML.

evidence.citation: University of Oxford Faculty of English, Ten-Minute Book Club: Henryson's Cock and Jasp from the Morall Fabillis
evidence.summary: Supports the rhyme-royal description and literary framing of the Cock and Jasp within the Morall Fabillis.

evidence.citation: HenFabl lineage evidence for Makculloch, Bannatyne, Asloan, Gibb, Harley, Charteris, and Bassandyne
evidence.summary: Supports early attestation, attribution, survival boundaries, source-content limits, and the distinction between the Asloan original and Gibb's transcript.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item 0243 record and cached TEI header
evidence.summary: Supports the legacy 1480-1489 date, Scots language, tentative Fife code, source-edition boundary, normalization history, and explicit uncertainty about the constructed base text.

evidence.citation: British Library catalog record for Harley MS 3865
evidence.summary: Supports 13-fable contents, two prologues, 2,968 numbered lines, 416 seven-line and seven eight-line stanzas, manuscript date, and physical source boundary.

evidence.citation: Direct comparison of OTA plain text, U-M XML, and Smith/Bassandyne representations
evidence.summary: Confirms Middle Scots primary language, embedded Latin phrases, case conversion, inherited spellings, and the distinction between textual language and modern English metadata.

open_questions.question: Can the work's composition be dated more tightly than the late fifteenth century or securely localized to Dunfermline/Fife?
next_steps: Prefer a current specialist argument grounded in internal allusions, Henryson's documented career, and witness chronology before narrowing either range or place.

open_questions.question: Do the thirteen fables constitute one authorially unified and ordered collection, or does the familiar sequence partly reflect early print transmission and editorial compilation?
next_steps: Retain the represented 13-fable sequence as the cataloging unit while tracking new stemmatic or codicological scholarship on independent circulation, witness order, and print editorial intervention.
notes: content_structure_status complete means all sections of the represented sequence are enumerated, not that the unity debate is resolved.

open_questions.question: Which Smith stream supplied each reading in OTA 0243 and the U-M continuous text?
next_steps: Locate OSTA/DOST production notes or perform a systematic machine collation against all five Smith streams and the Bassandyne facsimile.
notes: The current evidence combines a BASS PR label and Bassandyne title, Harleian odd-page markers, and a Charteris-only unnumbered stanza.

open_questions.question: Which exact Latin and vernacular exemplars did Henryson use for each individual fable?
next_steps: Model individual analogue relations only when supported by fable-specific textual comparison; do not turn broad Aesopic affiliation into direct physical lineage.

notes: Henryson's authorship is modeled separately from Aesop's traditional authority and from the modern editor, transcribers, printers, scribes, encoders, and repositories.
The preferred date is late fifteenth century, perhaps 1470s-1480s. The XML's 1450 and OTA's 1480-1489 are legacy metadata points, not interchangeable certainties.
Dunfermline is a well-supported associated place for Henryson, while Fife as a place of composition remains tentative.
The 2,975-line total is an XML count: 2,968 inherited numbered lines plus one unnumbered seven-line stanza. It should not be mistaken for a newly established canonical lineation.
The XML's second DIV2 labeled prologue inside The Lion and the Mouse is the 168-line narrative; this manifest repairs the intellectual labeling without modifying the source.

Complete source-object caches are recorded in lineage: Bassandyne's unique print, Smith's full reprint volume, the 454-canvas Bannatyne main manuscript, and the 628-canvas surviving Asloan manuscript. Their work portions and historical losses remain explicit.

No reusable source-evaluation or modeling problem was found beyond the project skill's existing rules on mixed electronic bases, provider-relative completeness, witness/transcript separation, discontinuous work portions, and rights-layer separation.
